name: Sine Wave
spectral_profile: Single vertical spike at frequency f
spectrogram_appearance: Single bright horizontal line at fundamental frequency
notes: The simplest periodic waveform. All other periodic waveforms can be decomposed into sums of sine waves.

name: Square Wave
spectral_profile: Spikes at f, 3f, 5f, 7f... decreasing at -20 dB/decade
spectrogram_appearance: Horizontal lines at odd multiples; bright fundamental, dimming higher harmonics
notes: Clarinet approximates a square wave due to odd-mode resonances in cylindrical bore.

name: Triangle Wave
harmonic_content: Odd harmonics only, falling at -40 dB/decade
spectral_profile: Spikes at f, 3f, 5f... decreasing at -40 dB/decade (much faster than square)
spectrogram_appearance: Odd harmonic lines, rapidly diminishing in brightness
visual_shape: Linear ramps up and down, forming a zig-zag
notes: Softer timbre than square wave due to -40 dB/decade harmonic rolloff vs -20 dB/decade for square.

name: Sawtooth Wave (Rising / Ramp)
spectral_profile: Spikes at all integer multiples f, 2f, 3f... decreasing at -20 dB/decade
spectrogram_appearance: Dense stack of harmonic lines, bright fundamental with gradual dimming
visual_shape: Slow linear rise, abrupt drop — like a shark fin
notes: The richest standard waveform. Foundation of subtractive synthesis. Violin bowing mechanism produces a sawtooth-like waveform.

name: Sawtooth Wave (Falling / Reverse)
harmonic_content: All harmonics (both odd and even), phase inverted
timbre: Bright, buzzy — same as rising but with opposite phase spectrum
spectral_profile: Same magnitude spectrum as rising sawtooth, different phase
spectrogram_appearance: Identical to rising sawtooth (magnitude spectrogram phase-agnostic)
notes: Magnitude spectrum identical to rising sawtooth; differs in phase spectrum. Perceptually nearly identical to rising sawtooth.

name: Pulse Wave / PWM
formula: y(t) = A if t mod T < D·T else -A   (D = duty cycle, 0<D<1)
harmonic_content: All harmonics; null at multiples of 1/D
timbre: Hollow at 50% duty; increasingly thin/nasal as duty cycle deviates
spectral_profile: All harmonics present except nulls at multiples of 1/D (e.g., D=0.25 → nulls at 4f, 8f, 12f...)
spectrogram_appearance: Harmonic stack with characteristic gaps; gap position shifts as duty cycle modulates
notes: At D=0.5 (50% duty cycle) equals a square wave. PWM synthesis sweeps duty cycle over time, creating a characteristic sweeping formant effect.

name: White Noise
formula: y(t) = random sample from N(0, σ²) at each sample
spectral_profile: Flat — equal energy across all frequencies (up to Nyquist)
spectrogram_appearance: Uniform bright noise across all frequencies and all time
notes: Power Spectral Density is constant: S(f) = N₀/2. Autocorrelation = δ(τ). Gaussian distribution in amplitude.

name: Pink Noise (1/f Noise)
timbre: Balanced hiss — more bass than white noise
fourier_series: Not applicable — stochastic with defined PSD
spectrogram_appearance: Brighter at low frequencies, gradually darker toward high frequencies
visual_shape: Irregular fluctuations with more low-frequency content
notes: Pink noise appears throughout nature: heartbeat variability, stock prices, music dynamics, neural firing. Perceived as most 'natural' noise color.

name: Brown Noise (Brownian / Red Noise)
harmonic_content: Heavily bass-weighted; high frequencies very attenuated
fourier_series: Not applicable — stochastic (random walk / Brownian motion)
spectrogram_appearance: Predominantly bright at low frequencies; quickly darkens to near-zero at high frequencies
visual_shape: Very slow, heavy fluctuations like ocean swells
notes: Named after Brownian motion (random walk). Each sample is the previous sample plus a random step, naturally integrating white noise.

name: Impulse / Dirac Delta
formula: y(t) = A if t=0 else 0   (discrete: y[n] = A·δ[n])
timbre: Click or pop (in practice, very short burst)
spectral_profile: Perfectly flat spectrum — all frequencies present at equal amplitude
spectrogram_appearance: Single bright vertical stripe across all frequencies at the moment of impulse
brightness: Maximum at moment of impulse, zero at all other times
notes: The Dirac delta is the impulse response of the identity system. Convolution with an impulse returns the original signal. Practical impulses are very short bursts (pistol shot, balloon pop) used in acoustic measurement.

name: Chirp / Frequency Sweep
harmonic_content: Single instantaneous frequency that changes over time
timbre: Sweeping tone, rising or falling pitch
spectral_profile: Linear or logarithmic diagonal line in spectrogram (depending on sweep type)
spectrogram_appearance: Diagonal bright line: ascending (f₁→f₂) or descending (f₂→f₁) chirp
visual_shape: Oscillation with accelerating or decelerating cycle rate
brightness: High — concentrated single-frequency energy at each instant
notes: Linear chirp: f(t) = f₁ + (f₂-f₁)·t/T. Log chirp: f(t) = f₁ · (f₂/f₁)^(t/T). Log chirp gives equal energy per octave.

name: Complex Harmonic Tone
harmonic_content: Multiple harmonics with specified amplitudes and phases
timbre: Rich, instrument-like; depends on harmonic amplitudes
spectral_profile: Multiple spikes at integer multiples of fundamental; varying heights
spectrogram_appearance: Horizontal harmonic stack with varying brightness per partial
visual_shape: Complex oscillation — sum of multiple sine waves
notes: The general model for all pitched musical instruments. Timbre is determined entirely by the relative amplitudes (Aₙ) and phases (φₙ) of harmonics. Phase has little perceptual effect due to Ohm's auditory law.
